title: Cardamom and orange cake
subtitle: A fragrant, citrus-lifted sponge with cardamom through the crumb
excerpt: Ground cardamom is briefly heated in the melted butter before going into the batter, which releases the spice's oils more effectively than stirring it in cold. Orange zest carries its fragrance through the crumb. The result is a lightly spiced, moist sponge that works equally well as a weekday cake or a dinner-party dessert. It bakes in about 35 minutes.
sourceNotes: Cardamom and orange are paired extensively in Scandinavian, Persian and Arab baking traditions. In Scandinavia, cardamom appears in Julekake and lussekatter; in the Middle East and North Africa it flavours cakes, pastries and coffee. The combination with orange is documented in Anissa Helou's 'Feast: Food of the Islamic World' (2018) and in Diana Henry's 'Simple' (2016).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze plain for up to 2 months. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps for 2 days at room temperature, wrapped in foil.

glossaryTerms.definition: Heating ground spice briefly in melted butter or warm oil before adding it to a batter or dough. Heat releases the volatile aromatic compounds from the spice more effectively than stirring the powder in cold, giving a more intense and rounded flavour in the finished bake without requiring a larger quantity. Stir the spice into the hot melted butter and leave for 1 to 2 minutes before using; it should smell noticeably more fragrant immediately. The technique works well with cardamom, cinnamon, ginger and spice blends.

content.text: : the cardamom goes into the hot melted butter for a minute before the butter cools and the rest of the ingredients are added. This step makes the spice flavour noticeably more present in the finished cake.

Use freshly ground cardamom from pods if you can; it has a brightness that pre-ground loses within a few months of opening. Crack the pods, discard the husks and grind the dark seeds in a spice grinder or with a pestle and mortar.

items.prepNote: zest of both, juice of 1 (about 50 ml)

content.text: Melt the 175 g butter in a small pan, then stir in the 2 tsp ground cardamom. Leave for 2 minutes, then set aside to cool to room temperature.

Heat the oven to 170 degrees C fan. Grease and line a 20 cm round cake tin. Whisk the 3 eggs and 200 g caster sugar together until pale and slightly thickened, about 2 minutes. Whisk in the cooled cardamom butter, the orange zest, 50 ml orange juice and 60 ml milk. Sift in the 225 g flour and 1.5 tsp baking powder and fold until smooth.

Pour into the prepared tin and bake for 32 to 38 minutes until the top is golden brown, the edges are pulling from the tin, and a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes, then on a rack. If glazing, mix the 100 g icing sugar with enough orange juice (about 1 tbsp) to make a thick, pourable glaze and drizzle over the cooled cake.

items.solution: The cardamom may be old and stale, or the blooming step was skipped. Replace pre-ground cardamom after 6 months; check by rubbing a pinch between your fingers - it should smell strongly aromatic. Freshly ground seeds from pods are consistently more potent.
The butter was too hot when the eggs were added, which partially cooked them and prevented them from providing lift. The cardamom butter must be fully cooled before being mixed with the eggs.

items.problem: Glaze soaks in rather than setting on the surface
items.solution: The glaze was applied to a warm cake. Let the cake cool completely before glazing; the surface needs to be at room temperature for the icing to set and hold its shape.
